name: Somatropin (HGH)
summary: Somatropin is recombinant human growth hormone (rhGH): a 191-amino-acid polypeptide identical to pituitary-derived human GH, produced via recombinant DNA technology. Unlike GH secretagogues (CJC-1295, ipamorelin, tesamorelin) that stimulate the body's own pituitary to release GH, somatropin directly supplies exogenous GH itself. It is grouped here under the GH-axis category for thematic clarity, but mechanistically it is the hormone, not a releaser of it.
mechanism:
Somatropin is a 191-amino-acid single-chain polypeptide (~22 kDa) with a sequence identical to endogenous human growth hormone, expressed recombinantly (commonly in E. coli or mammalian cells). Because it is the hormone itself, it bypasses the pituitary entirely: it does not act on the GHRH receptor or the ghrelin receptor the way secretagogues do, and it overrides the normal hypothalamic-pituitary feedback that limits endogenous GH pulses.

Somatropin binds a single GH receptor (GHR) that then dimerizes in the target-cell membrane, triggering JAK2/STAT5 intracellular signaling. Effects are both direct and indirect. Directly, GHR activation on adipose and other tissues promotes lipolysis and exerts anti-insulin (diabetogenic) metabolic effects. Indirectly, GH stimulates the liver and peripheral tissues to produce insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1), the main mediator of GH's anabolic and growth-promoting actions, including muscle protein synthesis, bone and connective-tissue growth, and tissue repair.

Because exogenous GH is delivered as a bolus rather than the body's natural pulsatile secretion, and because it does not respect normal negative feedback, supraphysiologic dosing can drive IGF-1 well above the physiologic range, which is the basis for both its anabolic effects and its safety concerns.
benefits: FDA-approved replacement therapy in diagnosed GH deficiency (pediatric and adult), normalizing IGF-1 and improving body composition, bone density, and lipid profile
Increases lean body mass and reduces fat mass via lipolysis plus IGF-1-mediated protein synthesis
Approved for several growth/wasting indications: Turner syndrome, Prader-Willi syndrome, SHOX deficiency, chronic renal insufficiency, short bowel syndrome, and HIV-associated wasting
Supports connective-tissue and bone remodeling, with reported recovery and repair effects through the GH/IGF-1 axis
May improve subjective energy, sleep quality, and quality of life when correcting a true deficiency
risks: Directly raises IGF-1 and can push it supraphysiologic with non-medical dosing - a theoretical driver of tumor growth; contraindicated in active malignancy
Diabetogenic: reduces insulin sensitivity and can cause hyperglycemia, impaired glucose tolerance, or new-onset type 2 diabetes, especially at higher doses
Contraindicated in acute critical illness (post-open-heart/abdominal surgery, multiple trauma, acute respiratory failure) where it increased mortality in trials
Off-label/non-medical use for bodybuilding, anti-aging, or athletic performance is not FDA-approved and is widely counterfeited; product quality varies
Prohibited at all times by WADA (peptide hormones / growth factors class) in competitive sport; distribution for non-medical use is illegal in many jurisdictions
Acromegaly-like features (bone, soft-tissue, and organ overgrowth) with chronic supraphysiologic dosing
sideEffects: Fluid retention: peripheral edema, joint stiffness and arthralgia, myalgia
Carpal tunnel syndrome and other compression-type paresthesias
Hyperglycemia / impaired glucose tolerance and insulin resistance
Injection-site reactions; lipoatrophy or tissue atrophy if the same site is overused (rotate sites)
Headache (evaluate for intracranial hypertension if severe/persistent)
Benign intracranial hypertension (papilledema, vision changes) - typically early in therapy
Anti-GH antibody formation (usually without loss of efficacy)

dosage.notes: Educational reference only, not advice. Medical GH-deficiency dosing is weight-based and titrated to IGF-1: adult start is typically <=0.2 mg/day (about 0.04 mg/kg/week max early), increased every 4-8 weeks to no more than ~0.08 mg/kg/week (often 0.2-0.5 mg/day in adults), with lower weight-based but higher mg/day pediatric dosing. GH is dosed by mass (mg) and sometimes IU (1 mg approximately equals 3 IU for modern rhGH). Non-medical anti-aging/physique use of 1-2+ mg/day is supraphysiologic, unapproved, and markedly increases side-effect and IGF-1 risk; lower, IGF-1-guided dosing is the conservative educational framing.
dosage.timing: Once daily by subcutaneous injection, typically in the evening / pre-sleep to loosely mimic the body's nocturnal GH pulse. Real adult GH-deficiency dosing is individualized and clinician-supervised, started low and titrated to a serum IGF-1 target (kept within the age-adjusted reference range, below the upper limit of normal), not set to a fixed number.
dosage.maxDose: No universal ceiling; dose is capped by the IGF-1 target rather than a fixed mg value. As a conservative educational reference, adult GH-deficiency dosing rarely needs to exceed ~1 mg/day and is usually far lower (~0.2-0.6 mg/day). Caution: somatropin is a powerful hormone - pushing dose to raise IGF-1 above the physiologic range risks acromegaly-like soft-tissue/organ overgrowth, worsening insulin resistance/hyperglycemia, fluid retention and arthralgia, and a theoretical tumor-growth concern. Higher is not better; the target is a normal IGF-1, not a maximal one.

protocols.label: Standard (adult GH-deficiency, IGF-1-titrated) - educational reference, clinician-individualized

steps.note: Low starting dose (~0.2 mg/day; ~0.1 mg/day if >60 yrs or prone to side effects). Evening/pre-sleep SUBQ. Baseline IGF-1 and glucose before starting.
Titrate up by ~0.1-0.2 mg every 4-8 weeks ONLY if IGF-1 is still below target and side effects (edema, arthralgia, glucose) are tolerable. Recheck IGF-1 before each increase.
Continue stepwise titration guided by serum IGF-1 (age-adjusted reference range, below ULN), clinical response, and side effects - not by a fixed schedule.

Typical adult maintenance commonly lands around ~0.4-0.6 mg/day once IGF-1 is in range. Hold or reduce dose if IGF-1 reaches target or side effects appear. Dose is the value that normalizes IGF-1, which differs per person.

frequency: Once daily, commonly in the evening to loosely mimic the nocturnal GH pulse; some pediatric regimens divide or vary timing per product labeling.
halfLife: Injected drug: terminal half-life ~20 minutes after IV, but ~2-4 hours after subcutaneous injection due to slow depot absorption. Biological effects last far longer because GH raises IGF-1, which has a half-life on the order of ~12-15 hours and sustains downstream anabolic signaling.
cycleLength: In approved deficiency therapy it is taken continuously (often indefinitely) with periodic IGF-1 and glucose monitoring and dose titration. Non-medical users sometimes run multi-month blocks, but there is no validated 'cycle' - benefits regress off-drug and IGF-1/glucose should be monitored throughout.

reconstitution.notes: Many pharmaceutical somatropin products ship as pre-filled pens or pre-mixed cartridges and do NOT require user reconstitution - follow the specific product's instructions. For lyophilized vials (commonly 5 mg or 10 mg), adding 1 mL bacteriostatic or supplied diluent to a 5 mg vial yields 5 mg/mL, so 0.2 mg = 0.04 mL (4 units on a U-100 syringe). Add diluent slowly down the vial wall and swirl gently; never shake. Dosing in mg and converting to IU (~3 IU/mg) is product-specific - verify against the label.

storage: Lyophilized vials: refrigerate at 2-8 C and protect from light. After reconstitution (and for pre-mixed pens in use), refrigerate at 2-8 C and use within the product-specified window (commonly ~14-28 days, varies by brand). Do not freeze; freezing denatures the protein. Discard if the solution is cloudy or has particulates.
contraindications: Acute critical illness due to complications after open-heart or abdominal surgery, multiple accidental trauma, or acute respiratory failure
Prader-Willi syndrome in patients who are severely obese, have a history of upper-airway obstruction or sleep apnea, or have severe respiratory impairment (risk of sudden death)
Active proliferative or severe non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy
Closed epiphyses when used for growth promotion in pediatric patients
Known hypersensitivity to somatropin or excipients; benzyl-alcohol-preserved formulations should not be used in neonates/premature infants
Pregnancy and breastfeeding (use only if clearly needed; generally avoided)

interactions.note: Somatropin's primary downstream mediator IS IGF-1 - GH directly raises endogenous IGF-1. Stacking exogenous IGF-1 (IGF-1 LR3) on top of GH compounds IGF-1 exposure, amplifying both the anabolic effect and the risks (hypoglycemia from IGF-1, plus GH's hyperglycemia, tissue/organ overgrowth, and theoretical tumor-growth concern). Monitor IGF-1 closely; this is high-risk redundancy rather than clean synergy.

CJC-1295 is a GHRH analog that works by stimulating the pituitary to release endogenous GH. Adding it to exogenous somatropin is largely redundant - you are already supplying GH directly - and stacking the two compounds IGF-1 load and GH-related side effects (fluid retention, joint symptoms, insulin resistance) with little additive benefit.

Ipamorelin is a selective GH secretagogue (ghrelin-receptor agonist) that triggers the body's own GH pulse. On top of exogenous somatropin this is mechanistically redundant; the marginal endogenous pulse adds little while increasing total GH/IGF-1 exposure and side-effect burden.

Tesamorelin is a GHRH analog that raises endogenous GH/IGF-1. Combining it with exogenous somatropin is redundant and additive on IGF-1, increasing edema, arthralgia, and insulin-resistance risk without a clear rationale over choosing one approach.

Opposing metabolic effects on glucose: somatropin reduces insulin sensitivity and raises blood glucose, whereas the GLP-1/GIP agonist tirzepatide improves glycemic control. The combination is not inherently dangerous but makes glucose effects harder to interpret - monitor closely, since GH may blunt incretin glycemic benefit and antidiabetic dose needs can shift.

Like other incretin agonists, retatrutide improves insulin sensitivity and glycemia, directly opposing somatropin's diabetogenic effect. Watch glucose carefully if combined; the two pull glycemic control in opposite directions and complicate dose titration of either.

GH antagonizes insulin and reduces insulin sensitivity, so insulin and oral antidiabetic requirements often rise during somatropin therapy. Monitor blood glucose and adjust antidiabetic dosing accordingly.

Somatropin can inhibit 11-beta-HSD1, lowering cortisol conversion and potentially unmasking central hypoadrenalism; conversely supraphysiologic glucocorticoids blunt GH's growth-promoting effect. Glucocorticoid replacement may need adjustment when starting GH.
